answer.text: Selling a call option on an asset you already own (long stock + short call) — it generates premium income but caps the upside at the strike price. It works best when you expect modest price movement
Buying a call option while simultaneously buying the underlying stock
Selling a call on a stock you don't own (naked call writing)
Buying both a call and put on the same stock simultaneously

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Covered call = Long stock + Short call. The premium earned enhances return if the stock stays below K. If stock rises above K, the shares are called away at K — upside is capped. Max profit = (K - Stock price) + Premium. Max loss = Stock price - Premium (stock falls to zero). **The Trap:** Buying a call WITH stock is not a standard covered call — it's a leveraged long. Naked call writing has unlimited risk. Buying call AND put is a straddle. **Cross-Exam Context:** Covered calls exist in equity (VIII Ch5) and can be adapted for currency hedging (writing calls on currency holdings). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Covered calls are the most popular income-generating option strategy — they work well in rangebound or mildly bullish markets. The risk is missing a big rally when the stock gets called away.
